result:
I want to write about the moment the house goes quiet after the last suitcase thumps down the hall, when the click of the door is still echoing in the stairwell and the refrigerator suddenly sounds like a freight train because no one is talking over it. That is the moment the rooms enlarge, not metaphorically but measurably: the walls recede a millimeter or two, the ceiling rises a cautious centimeter, as if the building itself inhales now that it no longer has to accommodate the daily weather of other bodies. I stand in the hallway and feel the air rearrange itself around my single pair of lungs, the way a forest must feel when the campers pack up and the embers stop hissing.  

The first thing I do is leave the mess exactly where it is: the coffee ring on the pine table, the sock behind the bathroom door, the fridge magnet twisted askew. They look like archaeological evidence, proof that the hours before this one actually happened, that I did not hallucinate the quarrel about the boarding pass or the laughing fit when the jar of cumin exploded. If I tidy too quickly I will erase the only footprints I still have of the people who have just walked out of the montage that was our life together. So I let the crumbs stay, tiny asteroids on the dark counter, and I circle them like a museum guard doing rounds.  

Time behaves differently once no one is waiting for you to join them at a gate, a beach, a restaurant table. It folds, origami-style, into little compartments you can slip into your pocket: ten minutes becomes a palpable object, a smooth worry-stone you can turn over while you stare at the spines of books you suddenly have the leisure to hate. You notice that the afternoon light has a color you cannot name, something between the inside of a peach and the sound of a distant church bell, and you realize you have been standing in the same spot long enough for the sun to move the width of one floorboard. You have become your own sundial, and the shadow you cast is the only clock that matters.  

I make coffee for one and forget to drink it. Instead I carry the mug from room to room, a secular censer, swinging it gently so the aroma drifts into corners that still smell of someone else’s shampoo. In the bedroom I open the wardrobe and confront the hangers left in hysterical formation: three identical black T-shirts, a lone yellow dress that looks embarrassed to be seen alone. I close the door again immediately, ashamed of my voyeurism, and promise the clothes I will not ghost-write their futures today. They can hang there a little longer, practicing the art of patience, learning how to sway when the window is cracked and the evening breeze slips in like a curious cat.  

Outside, the city continues its loud conversation with itself, but from inside the apartment it sounds dubbed into a language I almost recognize. Car horns are syllables, sirens are vowels lengthened by grief or desire. I press my forehead to the cool glass and try to lip-read the intentions of strangers who will never know they have been enlisted as extras in the silent movie of my reclamation. A woman below argues with a parking meter, gesticulating as though the machine could be shamed into mercy. I decide she is arguing on my behalf, that she is the chorus in a play I did not know I was starring in, and when she finally kicks the meter and storms off, I feel oddly vindicated, as if the universe has leaned in to whisper, See, resistance is still possible.  

Eventually hunger becomes a polite knock instead of a battering ram, and I discover that the refrigerator has been left with the exact ingredients for a meal no one in the departed group ever liked: lentils, fennel, the misshapen carrot languishing in the crisper like an unwanted wand. I chop slowly, reverently, as though each piece is a sentence I am learning to pronounce in a new dialect. The onion is the hardest chapter; it insists on commentary, stinging my eyes until I supply the tears it demands. I let them fall without wiping, a small baptism for the cutting board. When the pot begins to simmer, the sound is a congregation murmuring amen, and I am suddenly the pastor, the parishioner, and the visiting doubter all at once.  

While dinner burbles, I move to the piano that has spent the last year pretending to be decorative furniture. Its lid is closed, its teeth clenched, as if it made a promise long ago to forget how to bite. I sit anyway, lift the cover, and press middle C. The note rings out like a bell calling sailors back from a shipwreck; it is more forgiving than I remember. I play nothing in particular, just a slow truce between my ten pardoned fingers and the eighty-one obedient beasts inside. Each key I strike is a small evacuation of silence, and when I finally close the lid again the hush that returns feels altered, like a forest after birdsong has proven it is still alive.  

Night arrives without the usual negotiations about which movie to stream. I draw the curtains and the room shrinks back to human proportions, walls returning to their contracted selves now that darkness has removed the possibility of horizon. I light the candle someone once bought as a joke—Scent: Thunderstorm—and watch the flame practice its tiny yoga poses on the wick. The shadow it throws against the wall is gigantic, a distorted echo of the small certainty I have decided to allow myself. I speak out loud, just to verify that speech still works when there is no echo of laughter or objection: “I am still here.” The sentence hangs, curls, becomes part of the ceiling, a cobweb I will not sweep away.  

Before bed I walk the perimeter like a guard changing shifts, touching the spine of each doorframe, the corners of tables, the lip of the sink. These are the tactile bookmarks of my new codex, the braille of singularity. I save the front door for last, resting my palm on its cool metal skin as if feeling for a pulse. Through it pass days that will arrive unannounced, bearing groceries, postcards, storms, second thoughts, maybe even the tentative knock of someone who has not yet learned the password. I tell the door thank you, an incantation I never thought to utter when arrivals and departures were merely punctuation in a run-on sentence.  

In the dark bedroom I lie diagonal across the mattress, a five-pointed star claiming the whole sky of the sheet. The silence is no longer an accusation; it is a blanket someone has finally tucked around my shoulders without asking if I am cold. I listen to the faint ticking of the wristwatch left on the windowsill, a sound previously drowned by heartbeats that were not my own. Each tick is a seed planted in the acreage of night, and I am the curious farmer who has decided to stay up late and watch them grow. Somewhere between one second and the next I understand that solitude is not the opposite of company but its continuation under a different treaty, a country whose passport is issued only after you have stood in the hush of your own kitchen and survived the moment the house went quiet.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
